This is something really interesting and I think it would be valuable to have both boto and boto3 backends in Depot. As Minio is compatible with boto3 I think we should have a S3 storage based on Boto3 and let people use it with Minio. 

Regarding the `encrypt_key` that tells S3 to encrypt the object server side, see http://boto.cloudhackers.com/en/latest/ref/s3.html#boto.s3.key.Key.set_contents_from_string

Best solution that comes to my mind is provide an `BotoBucketDriver` and `Boto3BucketDriver`, abstract lib specific behaviour there and let the user choose which one to use as a parameter to `S3Storage`. By default we should keep boto for backward compatibility but using boto3 should be the matter of just turning on an option.

Then we should provide a `MinioStorage` that just inherits from `S3Storage`  and just enable the `Boto3BucketDriver` for convenience for new users that should just have to say _"how do I use depot with minio? Ah just use the MinioStorage"_.

Looks like that best solution for `virtualenv` problems is to call it as module:
```
python -m virtualenv ...
```
or in python code replace:
```python
check_call(["virtualenv", ...])
```
with:
```python
check_call([sys.executable, '-m', "virtualenv", ...])
```

Trying to do excessive `release()` on BoundedSemaphore would break - I want the bound to be there but also to be able to potentially increase it.

Additionally, resizing could possibly have different semantics when resizing it to be smaller; would it block there too or just stop any new tasks from acquiring the semaphore until it goes above the high water mark again? I would've PR'd I didn't know how to figure this one out so it stopped me.
I could certainly expose the value with a property perhaps.  What do you have in mind for resizing it beyond using the `acquire()` and `release()` methods on it?

Just came across pep-0475:

> System call wrappers provided in the standard library should be 
> retried automatically when they fail with EINTR , to relieve application 
>code from the burden of doing so.
> 
> By system calls, we mean the functions exposed by the standard C library 
> pertaining to I/O or handling of other system resources.

it looks like people are already thinking along the right lines.

It also has a good summary of the different signals that windows listens to:

I think this needs more study.    If all of this is running on a single machine, there are two many weird quirks of scheduling that could be in play regarding threads, processes, and everything else.    I'm also not sure that a client hitting the server at maximum speed through the loopback interface represents a scenario that would happen for real, but then again, I honestly don't know.

I just know that performing all sorts of extra system calls and scheduling around I/O isn't free.  So, all things equal,  I don't want to unnecessarily cripple the performance of curio if I don't have to.    

Just as a note: I've seen similar "starvation" effects simply programming with threads (no async).  A lot of this may be tied up in the scheduler of the OS.   

I'll have more to say about this later.... have to step away to pick up kids though.

Well. VDF is just KVs. I see no reason to not be able have `"0"` as the root node. I haven't seen a single file that does that. It's more of a consistency thing. The serialization part can easily be fixed.

```
if isinstance(value, list):
    value = enumerate(value)
```
I guess for deserialization you just check to see if the first subnode is `0` and turn into a `list`.
I've been thinking of adding that to my own `VDF` module. Essentially, deserialized data from the webapi will end up exactly the same, regardless of format being `json` or `vdf`. It makes sense.

Thanks for the awesome turnaround on that. I tried it today and unfortunately something I didn't notice initially has reared up to make this not helpful. It turns out that Django calls xgettext to do the formatting and the interpretation of `--no-wrap` is different than what you implemented. The way xgettext interprets it is that option only affects the msg lines. The line numbers are still constrained by the page width.

The [django makemessage command](https://code.djangoproject.com/browser/django/branches/releases/1.3.X/django/core/management/commands/makemessages.py)  calls:

    194                 cmd = (
    195                     'xgettext -d %s -L Perl %s --keyword=gettext_noop '
    196                     '--keyword=gettext_lazy --keyword=ngettext_lazy:1,2 '
    197                     '--keyword=pgettext:1c,2 --keyword=npgettext:1c,2,3 '
    198                     '--from-code UTF-8 --add-comments=Translators -o - "%s"' % (
    199                         domain, wrap, os.path.join(dirpath, thefile)
    200                     )

Where `wrap` is either `--no-wrap` or an empty string.

The [xgettext command](http://www.gnu.org/software/gettext/manual/gettext.html#xgettext-Invocation) says:

    ‘-w number’
    ‘--width=number’
    Set the output page width. Long strings in the output files will be split across multiple lines
    in order to ensure that each line's width (= number of screen columns) is less or equal to 
    the given number. 
    ‘--no-wrap’
    Do not break long message lines. Message lines whose width exceeds the output page width 
    will not be split into several lines. Only file reference lines which are wider than the output 
    page width will be split.
 
This means that in practice the file diffs are still different. I guess the only workable way right now would be to break at the exact spot that xgettext defaults to (which I haven't figured out yet). 

Otherwise you would have to only wrap msgstr at the `ROSETTA_POFILE_WRAP_WIDTH` width and wrap everything else at whatever the xgettext default width or perhaps change django such that the page width is configurable.

Just to follow up, the pattern matching should allow for some special values, from page 673 of the 2012 standard:

    Date ::= [APPLICATION 10] OCTET STRING (SIZE(4)) -- see 20.2.12
    -- first octet     year minus 1900    X'FF' = unspecified
    -- second octet    month (1..14)      1 = January
                                          13 = odd months
                                          14 = even months
                                          X'FF' = unspecified
    -- third octet     day of month (1..34)
                                          32 = last day of month
                                          33 = odd days of month
                                          34 = even days of month
                                          X'FF' = unspecified
    -- fourth octet    day of week (1..7)
                                          1 = Monday
                                          7 = Sunday
                                          X'FF' = unspecified

No doubt there are many combinations of these special values that are meaningless, and some that are disallowed in some context but allowed in others, and some that are going to be ambiguous.

As for using https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601, since it doesn't support wildcard characters or day-of-week, that won't work well.  But making **str()** be more universal like "yyyy-mm-dd dow", I'm all for that.

Hi Andreas,

I love your module.  It's very easy to work with.  Thank you for sharing it.

I agree in principle that it's a sound idea to supply the font.  I 
searched for a decent open source font that included Chinese and 
couldn't find one.  I had high hopes for unifont-9.0.06.ttf from 
http://unifoundry.com/unifont.html, but it just looked like crap. If 
your google mojo is better than mine, fantastic, but I eventually gave 
up.  There are just too many Chinese characters, I suspect, for a lot of 
font developers.

What I did was have the program look for three different fonts.

  * "ARIALUNI.ttf" was installed on one of my Windows computers (on
    which I have Word) but was not on another (which doesn't have Word. 
    This leads me to guess that Word may have been the source of that
    font.  But maybe I've just internationalized my desktop better than
    my laptop.)

  * If that fails, "Arial Unicode.ttf" is installed on my osX 10.10.5
    computer (which does not have Word, so I'm guessing this is a
    standard system font on the Mac).

  * If that fails, it searches for "Arial.ttf", which I'm pretty sure is
    essentially universal on Windows and OS X / macOS, but which does
    not include all Unicode characters.  (Most European languages are
    supported.  Chinese and Arabic are both missing. These are my two
    test Unicode languages, as I have user-provided translations for my
    program.  I didn't check Japanese, Korean, etc.)  This leaves us no
    worse off than if we use DroidSansMono.

  * If that fails, the program uses the DroidSansMono font you supply.
    It was unclear to me if that font was freely distributable, and I
    haven't had time to check into that yet.

It's not a perfect methodology, but of course users always have the 
option to supply their own font of choice, which we can assume will 
include the characters they need.

I'll grant you, it's not a perfect solution, but it's the best I could 
come up with.  At least for my needs, I think it will work. Most of my 
customers are academics using university-supplied hardware which is 
likely to have Word installed.

David

This is a proposal for some xml to record conditional glyph substitutions in designspace.

  *   I'm not sure if this covers all the conditions allowed by OpenType 1.8
  *   this is not proposal for feature language.
  *   just covers single glyphs, not groups.

    <rules>

        <rule name="vertical bars" enabled="true">

            <sub name="cent" byname="cent.alt"/>

            <sub name="dollar" byname="dollar.alt"/>

            <condition tag="wght" operator=">" value="250.000000"/>

            <condition tag="wdth" operator="<" value="100"/>

            <condition tag="opsz" operator=">" value="40"/>

        </rule>

    </rules>

rules

rules element: groups all the rules. Contains one or more rule elements

rule

rule element: a single rule. Contains one or more sub elements, and one or more condition elements. All conditions must evaluate to true for the rule to be

  *   name: attribute, optional. String. Descriptive name for this rule.
  *   enabled: attribute, optional. Bool. If this rule should be evaluated or not.

sub

sub element: a single glyph to glyph substitution.

  *   name attribute, required. Name of the source glyph.
  *   byname attribute, required. Name of the replacement glyph.

condition

condition element: a single condition that has to evaluate to true for this rule to be valid.

  *   tag element: attribute, required. OpenType axis tag.
  *   operator attribute, required. Can be either < or >
  *   value attribute, required. Number.

> Send request params in the request body instead of the query string. 

Yes! With a caveat...

This sounds like a really good move for _POST_ requests. However the request body is ignored in GET requests (and is expected to be ignored in DELETE requests too) so the change needs to affect only POST and PUT requests.

Solution should use both `params` and `data` for the request, from there which is filled could be conditional on the request type, handled at the calling method level, etc.

This is all contingent on my assumption from a brief review of the [requests codebase](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py) that requests will not move the `data` values to `params` for GET requests. If I'm wrong on that this this can be ignored!

1. I'll add another test and see if I can prove it out.  I'm not sure \c is a good example to look at, because that's not part of the standard C escape character set.  So \c doesn't get escaped and you'll always see \\c (that's one backslash, followed by a C character) when you print it.  As opposed to \n where the behaviour would be different if you print a raw string r'\n' versus a normal string '\n'.

2. Here's the unit test (haven't pushed yet).  I've tested both raw and normal strings:

```python
    def test_pre_encoded1(self):
        hdrs = {"test": '\c\n'}
        
        self.protocol._escape_headers(hdrs)
        
        self.assertEqual('\\c\\n', hdrs['test'])
        
        print('pass 1 %s' % hdrs)
        self.protocol._escape_headers(hdrs)
        print('pass 2 %s' % hdrs)
        self.protocol._escape_headers(hdrs)
        print('pass 3 %s' % hdrs)
        
        print(hdrs)
        
    def test_pre_encoded2(self):

I assume it's some stomp1.0 legacy thing that's never been fixed, but given that even in stomp1.0 you could send arbitrary headers, I'm still not sure why anyone would come to the decision that overloading content-length behaviour was a good idea.

```python
    def setUp(self):

then your subclass can do

```python
    def _get_vcr(self):

I have seen this.
So this is not a regular proxy, but a forward proxy which simply redirects all web traffic from https://my_fwd_proxy:1234/* to https://exchangeserver/*

Essentially the difference in response in doing the direct call to exchangeserver vs my_fwd_proxy is from line 33: 

```
DEBUG:urllib3.connectionpool:https://my_frwd_proxy:1234 "POST /EWS/Exchange.asmx HTTP/1.1" 401 0
DEBUG:urllib3.connectionpool:https://my_frwd_proxy:1234 "POST /EWS/Exchange.asmx HTTP/1.1" 401 0
**DEBUG:urllib3.connectionpool:https://my_frwd_proxy:1234 "POST /EWS/Exchange.asmx HTTP/1.1" 401 0**
```

In the direct exchangeserver case this becomes a 401 -> 401 -> 200 OK. But with the proxy we get the chain 401 -> 401 -> 401 and it never authorizes.

@BendingUnit-22 Yeah, Praat makes a different between `Pitch` and `PitchTier` (and `PitchTier` is currently not yet a part of the Python API in Parselmouth), but...
- `PitchTier` does have ways of changing the points in there (cfr. http://parselmouth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples/pitch_manipulation.html, and other modifications like "Add point...", "Remove point...", "Modify interval...", "Formula..."; have a look at the Praat docs and menus for more: http://www.fon.hum.uva.nl/praat/manual/PitchTier.html)
- `Pitch` also has the Praat action "Down to PitchTier", which lets you convert a Praat `Pitch` object to `PitchTier`, which lets you do the following:
```Python
pitch = parselmouth.Sound("the_north_wind_and_the_sun.wav").to_pitch()
pitch_tier = parselmouth.praat.call(pitch, "Down to PitchTier")
print(pitch_tier)
```

No ramble, good to get feedback. The example of doing from 500 -> 1 million is a good point, but I thought about that. I think that every keyframe should/could have a set of vertices/triangles that will make interpolation possible from the current to the next, and from the current to the previous. Say, you have 4 frames, with 1, 50, 1000, and 10 000 vertices, the 1st frame would need to have 50 vertices, the 2nd frame 50 and 1000, the 3d 1000 and 10 0000 and the last 10 000. 
Now the filling of the buffers is done per frame change (which can be quite slow), I'd like to do that only once (possibly lazily). This requires a bit of refactoring of the code, and I think having no interpolation can be part of that, I don't think it needs to be messy, and the same can happen for the Scatter class.

On Sun, Jan 10, 2016 at 7:39 PM, Luke Hodkinson <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> Okay, I've implemented some changes to try and help correct this problem.
> I'll explain what I've done.
>
> So, the problem really boils down to the Python version of the geocoder
> not doing the same thing as the JavaScript one; when we do a lookup via JS
> it works properly, but not from Python. What I've implemented can be broken
> down into two parts:
>
>    1.
>
>    I've added a raw field to the address model. This stores the
>    unmodified value that gets passed in to the lookup routine in
>    django-address. This way we have the unmodified value that was
>    requested to be stored as an address, and also the value that Google thinks
>    the address should be.
>    2.
>
>    I've augmented the admin view to make it easier to identify these
>    bogus lookups and correct them using the JavaScript geocoder. In the list
>    display in the admin you'll be able to see a new column called
>    consistent. This just shows if the raw column matches the formatted
>    column. This is included so you can now sort the entries by whether or not
>    they have the value you expected them to be. Then, when you want to fix an
>    inconsistent address, you can use the inline lookup column to quickly find
>    the correct address and save it.
>
> Please feel free to update the code, but please bare in mind that the
> migrations have changed, so you'll need to start from scratch with your
> original data.
>
> And, as always, please let me know if (when) you encounter more problems.
> :)

I see three things that don't look right about this code sample. First, you don't seem to be calling your trigger() function anywhere (and that function also won't work). Second, I think your for-loop should look like:

```python
for k in jsone[0]:
    dicts[k] = [d[k] for d in jsone] # this should be inside, not outside the loop
```

Third, you are throwing away all the events that occur between ``stream_position`` and ``next_stream_position``. What you really want to do is process all those events, and then propagate the ``next_stream_position``.

However, there is no need to do that manually. You should use the ``generate_events_with_long_polling`` method on the ``Events`` class. See https://github.com/box/box-python-sdk/blob/master/boxsdk/object/events.py#L105

Your code can look something like:

```python
for event in client.events().generate_events_with_long_polling(initial_stream_position):
    trigger(event)
```

In general, there's no `fancy` stuff you need to do to install on windows. We have pre-complied and packed to pip wheel so everyone should be able to install it using `pip install mxnet`. 
The windows version was available since 0.11 several months ago, and it is considerably stable because we do have a forum that have users reporting the installation on windows version. Recently it goes alright with very little complaining about installation problems.

Unfortunately, windows may sometimes missing some fundamental dependencies, such as visual c++ runtime, or CUDA runtime which must be installed separately by users, not to mention its complicated 
PATH management, making it hard to diagnose without experience. 

If you still have problems using mxnet-cu80, you can first try uninstall it, then `pip install mxnet` and try it to see if the CPU version works. If not, try install visual c++ 2015 redistributed package to enable c++ runtime on windows. If CPU version is good, then there must be some problem with the CUDA installation. You can test with CUDA samples provided by nvidia, or check the PATH again, mxnet do search for PATH so if should be fine just add them to PATH.

>I would consider argparse a given and not go back.

Indeed, and we have always used argparse. I wouldn't propose dropping argparse.

> In my vendorized copy actually I now use a flag to disable old eval-code completely.

That makes sense, and I would encourage you to continue to do that. If in your application all configurable traits are known at the time of constructing the parser, you can do a much better job than the traitlets default, even what we have now in master. For instance, you should not be using `type=text` for anything but actual text types, instead using `type=int` for Integers, and proper actions for containers, etc. Right now, I think we have a bit of a mess in the middle that's complicated by trying to preserve old behavior, while not achieving the goal of truly making the most of argparse. A parser that completely ignores the `--Class.trait=value` would be greatly simpler than what we have, it just can't be the default for Application. Nothing in the traitlets package prevents you from having the argument parsing you would prefer.

It's totally reasonable to use a different CLI parser in your own application, and I think you probably should unless you have the same goals as IPython. There's no reason that every possible parsing pattern needs to ship in traitlets.config, whose primary goal is to serve as a base for IPython/Jupyter applications.

This is the problem that I see with what we have in master:

    ipython --Class.dict_trait a=5

behaves differently, depending on whether Class is in IPython's preconfigured class list or not. That's not a good thing, and I think we must fix that before releasing 5.0. One of the primary goals of the IPython config system is that all objects, IPython's and those of any extension, are exposed the *same way* on the CLI and in every config file. This is not the case in master.

That doesn't need to extend to the aliases, which can get some help because the traits to which they map are required to be known. I think it's fine to have `-D a=5`, but I *don't* think that should extend to `--Class.d a=5` unless it is done in a way that extends to `Class.d` where `Class` is unknown at parsing time.

Yeah, that's the tricky part.
It's easy enough to detect that the message has a thrown keyid. If there isn't another PKESK without a thrown keyid that definitely matches, I think it'd be more natural for users if we just try to act on that, instead of making them check and remember to add a kwarg to get us to do the right thing.

As for behavior, currently it [raises a PGPError](https://github.com/SecurityInnovation/PGPy/blob/ad6905d57a0918e53c0f573bea70598d7de84d33/pgpy/pgp.py#L2232) if it doesn't have a keyid match to either the primary key or one of the subkeys, so if we try our key and all subkeys with Encrypt* usage flags and none of them work, I think it'd be most consistent to still raise PGPError to indicate failure.

Maybe it is cleanest to move the logic [in that conditional](https://github.com/SecurityInnovation/PGPy/blob/ad6905d57a0918e53c0f573bea70598d7de84d33/pgpy/pgp.py#L2222) to its own method (maybe `def _can_decrypt(self, message)`) that returns a collection of keyids that are able to attempt decrypting the message. Then, we can call that in `PGPKey.decrypt`, try decrypting with each (sub)key in the sequence, and either return the first successfully decrypted PGPMessage, or raise PGPError if none of them worked (or, by extension, if the collection returned was empty).

@jamesls Strictly speaking the current paginators are broken and are a regression over not having them. Since we make no attempt to aggregate the results it's the same as getting the first page with no next token. 

Here are some options:
* Remove the broken paginators (backwards incompatible)
* Support aggregate maps in pagination which is hard as different services expect different aggregation behavior. It should be clear that support for these existing paginators is an exception
* White list these paginators in the test (not ideal, the paginators are still broken) 

I think the ideal behavior is: provide the correct aggregation for paginators we already 'support' and whitelist them in the tests. I think map based pagination is strange enough that we still want to be failing tests when they are introduced in the future.

When wrapping a C structure:
```C
struct my_cstruct {
   ...
   ...
}
```
we define a Python type `my_ctype`[1] which is used in the prototyping. In addition the cwrap code will automatically define the auxillary types `my_ctype_ref` and `my_ctype_obj` the latter two are *only* used as return values. The `my_ctype_ref` means that the Python object holds a reference to the underlying C structure, but is not involved in the management of the underlying C structure. It is not implemented that way - but semantically you could say it is a weak reference[2]. The alternative `my_ctype_obj` means that the Python object takes ownership of the underlying C structure, and will call the underlying `xxx_free( )` function when the wrapping Python object goes out of scope.

The *parent* in this context does *not* imply the wrapping Python object, but rather a container of some sort. The typical situation is that the container is implemented in C and when the `container_free()` function is called in C it will also destroy the content. In the situation where Python manages the container, and we also have references to elements in the container it is essential that we do not call `container_free( )` as long as there are active child objects - to protect against this we add a reference from the child to the container - "artifically" increasing the reference count of the parent. 

[This test](https://github.com/Statoil/libecl/blob/master/python/tests/ecl_tests/test_ecl_file.py#L161) tests the parent stuff. What happens here is:

1. We [open a EclFile](https://github.com/Statoil/libecl/blob/master/python/tests/ecl_tests/test_ecl_file.py#L34) and get references to `EclKw` instances owned by the `EclFile`. The `EclKw` instances set a parent "pointer" back to the `EclFile`.

2. We let the `EclFile`instance go out of scope - without the parent mechanism the `EclFile` instance would now be closed, and would invalidate the `EclKw` instances.

3. Finally we verify that the `EclKw` instances are still valid.

> On the other hand, I think letting the parent being an arbitrary (python reference to) object brings in a huge risk of causing circular dependencies 

I agree - and there might of course be such problems buried in the code; but it has worked quite well for several years.

[1]: The names `my_cstruct` and `my_ctype` are *completely independent*.
[2]: Have not thought about it before - but if where possible to implement using a Python `weakref`that would certainly be cool.

Coming back to this after the break, and I think that if we can agree on some useful unixy command style, it makes sense to do that now, next to the functions that already exist as one-to-one API calls.

### dataset uri scheme

In order to do things like `rm` and `cp` we need a uri scheme to identify datasets and features within datasets. There's some precedence for this in other parts of the stack, so I would propose a scheme like:

```
mapbox://datasets/:user/:datasetid/:featureid
```

### proposed commands

- **mapbox datasets cat {uri}**: Make multiple API calls to print all the features in a dataset, or a single feature. Supports `limit`, `start` and `reverse` arguments, as well as output args `sequence` and `rs` like https://github.com/mapbox/cligj#example.

- **mapbox datasets rm {uri}**: Remove an individual feature from a dataset, or the entire dataset

- **mapbox datasets cp {source} {destination}**: Here `source` and `destination` are either uris or file paths. Replace the destination with the content of the source, making all the appropriate API requests to completely overwrite a dataset if that's the intention. Allow `-` for the source if features are to be fed via stdin, while `-` as destination indicates output pushed to stdout.

What I'm not sure about is how to differentiate an "append to dataset" command in a unixy kind of way. Any good ideas? @sgillies does this align at all with what you were thinking about when you originally made this suggestion?

`sudo rm /usr/lib/python3.5/_sysconfigdata.py
sudo ln -fs /usr/lib/python3.5/plat-x86_64-linux-gnu/_sysconfigdata_m.py /usr/lib/python3.5/
sudo mv /usr/lib/python3.5/_sysconfigdata_m.py /usr/lib/python3.5/_sysconfigdata.py`

Quick run-down of changes:
 * `.travis/install`:
   * Borrowed from `cryptography`.  Uses `pyenv` to install `pypy`, if `TOXENV` is set to use `pypy`.
 * `.travis/run`:
   * Borrowed from `cryptography`.  Wraps commands to set up the environment as needed.  If `TOXENV` is is set to use `pypy`, that means calling `pyenv init -` and evaluating the output.
 * `.travis.yml`:
   * Use the `TOXENV` environment variable, which is known to `tox`, instead of `TOX_ENV`, which is not.
   * Use the [`trusty`](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/trusty-ci-environment) distro.  That gets newer Python versions, and makes `pyenv` happier.  This is also used by `cryptography`.
   * Add `PYPY_VERSION` to there environment when building with `pypy` so that `.travis/install` knows which version to install for testing against.
 * `tox.ini`:
   * Put quotes around `{toxinidir}/.travis/environment` to guard against spaces in the path.  This is pedantic, as it has no effect in this config, but generally safer.

The `Mask` class was actually @astrofrog's idea and came out of a discussion I had with him earlier today (he was visiting STScI).  It would be a new class within `regions` (but it would likely reuse some code from `Cutout2D`).

The `to_mask()` method currently returns a mask array and tuple slice, but the region objects know nothing about the data on which the mask will be applied (e.g. performing photometry).  If the region falls partially or completely off the image, then the returned `tuple slice` will be incorrect.  Each and every time the mask is applied to data, the boundary conditions would need to be checked.  By returning a `Mask` object, the `Mask` object could then handle the boundary conditions (centralizing such code in a single place).  Perhaps something like

```
from regions import CirclePixelRegion
region = CirclePixelRegion(...)
mask = region.to_mask()     # returns a mask object
data_subset = mask.apply(data)
```
where the `apply()` method would handle the boundary checking.

Currently you would need to do:

```
from regions import CirclePixelRegion
region = CirclePixelRegion(...)
mask, slice_x, slice_y = region.to_mask()

# need code here to check slice boundary conditions

data_subset = mask * data[slice_y, slice_x]
```

I can reproduce the failure when I use Pillow 1.7.8.  I suspect this is a bug in the old Pillow; there are several issues and pull requests related to PNG images with alpha channels from back in 2013, but I haven't figured out which one might be the relevant bug (assuming there is one).

Here's what Pillow does with the test file `'foo3x5x4indexed.png'`. The middle row of pixels are all transparent (check with your favorite image viewer).  `a[:, :, 3]` is the alpha channel of the image, so the middle row of `a[:, :, 3]` should be all 0.    With Pillow 1.7.8, the first two bytes in the second row of `a[:, :, 3]` are 255. 

*Pillow 1.7.8:*

```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: from PIL import Image

In [3]: im = Image.open('foo3x5x4indexed.png')

In [4]: a = np.array(im.convert('RGBA'))

In [5]: a[:, :, 3]
Out[5]: 
array([[255, 255, 255, 255, 255],
       [255, 255,   0,   0,   0],
       [255, 255, 255, 255, 255]], dtype=uint8)
```

*Pillow 2.1.0, 2.9.0 and 3.0.0:*
```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: from PIL import Image

In [3]: im = Image.open('foo3x5x4indexed.png')

In [4]: a = np.array(im.convert('RGBA'))

In [5]: a[:, :, 3]
Out[5]: 
array([[255, 255, 255, 255, 255],
       [  0,   0,   0,   0,   0],
       [255, 255, 255, 255, 255]], dtype=uint8)
```

I guess it isn't necessary to build nightlies, but it makes it play better with versioneer. I'm glad to keep the tag only on my local branch as this is all very experimental.

versioneer keeps track of how many commits you are ahead compared to a specific `tag`, for example `.dev0+103` would be 103 commits ahead of the `.dev0` tag.

I could tag nightlies version by day, but that might create a conflict when running from a cron job and somebody accepts a PR between two jobs. Then builds of different arch's may actually have different commits included even though they have the same version number.

@rgommers @jaimefrio As reported here, there is an inconsistency with scipy w.r.t interpolation around image edges.  When we hit a coordinate outside of the image and mode is set to 'constant' with `cval` 0, then we simply take that point to be zero and complete the interpolation.  Scipy, on the other hand, does not even try to complete the interpolation, but immediately returns `cval`.  Should this be considered a bug or a feature on the scipy end?  And if a feature, would it be feasible to also add a mode that does the same thing scikit-image currently does?

This is somehow expected. 

Short answer is that U+2047 https://www.compart.com/en/unicode/U+2047 is a sort of reserved token meaning unknown.

First, we no longer know the original surface string when converting the input into id sequences. 
So EncodeIds([0]) will not return "^^".

The workaround for this situation is either
1)  Decode 0 to  "<unk>"
2)  Decode 0 to empty string ""
3)  Decode 0 to other reserved string.

We don't want to show internal representation like <unk>, <s> as they are tricky for users. Returning empty string will make the debugging harder for developer.  So we now use a special token ?? which can be interpretable both for user and developer.

Any comments for this behavior are appreciated. 
It would be nicer to allow user change the behavior like --unk-surface=<unk>

Thank you.

I understand on the server-side what this error is saying:

> tower_cli.exceptions.MethodNotAllowed: The Tower server says you can't make a request with the POST method to that URL (http://localhost/api/v2/schedules/).

The server doesn't allow creating a schedule via the schedules list, it forces the client to do it via a sublist. For example:

```
tower-cli schedule create --enabled=false --job-template=1009 --rrule="DTSTART:20180718T155801Z RRULE:FREQ=MONTHLY;INTERVAL=1" --name="foo jt schedule" -v
*** DETAILS: Checking for an existing record. *********************************
GET http://localhost:8013/api/v2/job_templates/1009/schedules/
Params: [('name', u'foo jt schedule'), ('unified_job_template', 1009)]

*** DETAILS: Writing the record. **********************************************
POST http://localhost:8013/api/v2/job_templates/1009/schedules/
Data: {'enabled': False, 'unified_job_template': 1009, 'rrule': u'DTSTART:20180718T155801Z RRULE:FREQ=MONTHLY;INTERVAL=1', 'name': u'foo jt schedule'}

Resource changed.
== =============== ==================== ======= 
id      name       unified_job_template enabled 
== =============== ==================== ======= 
49 foo jt schedule                 1009   false
== =============== ==================== =======

Hi @harthur,

Thanks for using Amazon SageMaker ,

There are two SageMaker clients: the `AmazonSageMaker` client which is used to create and manage Training Jobs, Endpoints and such, and the `AmazonSageMakerRuntime` which is just used for predictions (with `InvokeEndpointRequest` in `transform()`).

Instead of injecting this client, you have to change the value of this var in the `RequestBatchIterator` singleton:

`RequestBatchIterator. sagemakerRuntime = mySageMakerRuntimeClient`

Why? Because `Spark` has to serialize tasks to send them to workers in the `mapPartition()` call in `transform()`, and these AWS clients aren't serializable. So instead of serializing the `RequestBatchIterator` directly, we serialize a factory method that creates a `RequestBatchIterator`.

https://github.com/aws/sagemaker-spark/blob/master/sagemaker-spark-sdk/src/main/scala/com/amazonaws/services/sagemaker/sparksdk/SageMakerModel.scala#L509

https://github.com/aws/sagemaker-spark/blob/master/sagemaker-spark-sdk/src/main/scala/com/amazonaws/services/sagemaker/sparksdk/transformation/util/RequestBatchIterator.scala#L228-L239

> The true y-max after applying a delta, max(y1 + δy1, y2 + δy2) may become not representable in OpenType’s “value + delta” mechanism, especially for a font with multiple axes.

This statement is only true for multiple axes.  For one axis, max(..) is always representable since max of multiple piecewise-linear functions is always piecewise-linear itself.

>     For most type designers they want to specify the vertical origin, rather than the top side bearing. However if they want to do that, the top side bearing, v + δv – max(y1 + δy1, y2 + δy2), becomes unrepresentable.

TSB is unused and irrelevant.  I recommend compilers / designers never setting TSB in VVAR.  VORG is relevant to layout only and should be set correctly.

Not much changed in the Python layer of gRPC Python between 1.4 and 1.6, but probably a lot changed underneath us in gRPC Core. I suspect that the real problem is that forking/multiprocessing/working across processes has not to date ever been supported by gRPC Core (unfortunately [this is the best citation I can find at the moment](https://github.com/grpc/grpc/issues/7951#issuecomment-260811550)), so while something may happened to have worked with 1.4 it silently broke with 1.6.

One workaround might be "delay invoking anything from gRPC until after forking/running in the child process", but I'm not sure.

Batching provides two features today:
a - hard routing affinity, i.e. all requests end up at the same backend
b - some transport efficiencies over vanilla HTTP/1.1

If all you cared about was (b) then that is solved more thoroughly with GRPC/HTTP2 without the need for additional APIs to be exposed.

(a) can provide cache-locality benefits but it can also reduce your SLA if you're not careful so the choice to leverage this behavior should be carefully made by individual APIs.

We generally recommend APIs that need batching to make it part of the API itself e.g.
https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/pubsub/v1/pubsub.proto#L186
or to start using streams. If you want hard routing affinity across a set of temporally spaced API calls you should use some kind of affinity key in a header. A 'batch' library method is not the right mechanism to expose these semantics.

settings_path = 'settings/settings.yaml'  # i.e. if settings.yaml is located in the subfolder `settings`

```

The converter needs to know that how many features are "entering" the pipeline. If all else fails (see below), then the converter makes attempts to ask this information from the first step of the pipeline. However, class `StandardScaler` does not specify the `num_features` (or similar) attribute.

Your script can be "fixed" in many ways, but the simplest would be to convert `X` and `y` arguments from Numpy arrays to `pandas.DataFrame` and `pandas.Series` objects:
```
from pandas import DataFrame, Series

X = DataFrame(X, columns = iris.feature_names[:2])
y = Series(y, name = "Species")
```

The added benefit of specifying feature and target names is that this information will be incorporated into "model schema" (otherwise, features would be called `x1`, `x2`, .., `x{n}`, and the target would be called `y`, which is not helpful in long term).

Alternatively, you may keep `X` and `y` arguments as Numpy arrays, and specity their column names as `PMMLPipeline.active_fields` and `PMMLPipeline.target_field` attributes:
```
clf_module.active_fields = np.array(iris.feature_names[:2])
clf_module.target_field = "Species"
```

I like where this is headed. I have not had a chance to do much actual testing with it. I do have few thoughts though. 

You are doing a recursive find for the objects which I think is going to be problematic when thinking about device group hierarchies. Furthermore, I don't think the library even considers the possibility of device group hierarchies currently. Which my 5 minutes of testing, it looks like it would not be a trivial thing to implement because the hierarchy is not constructed in the normal config tree. Object precedence with dev group hierarchy and even panorama vs local FW objects is going to effect how you search for objects. My initial thought is that you will have to recurse up the tree, not down it. Just thinking out loud.

Also, it looks like you are effectively overriding the strings/lists with the actual objects. Won't that effect the write functionality of the library or are you planning to handle that by just looking at the object name when building the xml tree?

Hi @meejah, thank you for looking into this.

The root of the issue lies in this portion of code in the wamp component: 
https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/component.py#L726-L751
which assumes  that the underlying protocol implementation has a `connectionLost()` method. That code wraps around `connectionLost()` in order to to "capture" its invocation and thus triggering the rejection of the future returned by `_connect_once()`.

The first problem is that the asyncio implementation of `WebSocketAdapterProtocol` has no method named `connectionLost`, but it has one named `connection_lost`, overridden from the `asyncio.Protocol` class, which is "Called when the connection is lost or closed" (https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-protocol.html#asyncio.Protocol).

So, the first change here: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/pull/1040/files#diff-05ecf29bd3a5812b4f0b10f58ff172bbR100 renames the old `connection_lost` as `connectionLost` and redirects  the former to the latter, working around the naming mismatch.

With this change in place, another problem emerges: the `on_connect_success()` callback (https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/component.py#L726-L751)  expects a `protocol` argument. That callback is invoked as part of a chain starting in `autobahn.asyncio.component._connect_transport()` (https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/asyncio/component.py#L264-L283). The problem is that both `loop.create_connection()` and `loop. create_unix_connection()` invocations return a `(transport, protocol)` pair, which clashes with the protocol argument expected by `on_connect_success()`. Here is where the change in autobahn/asyncio/component.py comes into play. I wrap the future in order to transform the tuple result into a single proto argument.

Finally, the exception wrapping: the docs for `BaseProtocol.connection_lost(exc)` (https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-protocol.html#asyncio-protocol) state that "The argument is either an exception object or None". At the end of the failure callback chain there is the `handle_callback_error()" function defined here: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/component.py#L512. When the `fail` argument (which is still the `exc` coming from `connection_lost`) is None, we fall here: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/component.py#L543, where `txaio.failure_message(fail)` isn't much happy to receive a None argument.

I was just able to build a working psycopg2 on my system by using `rpath`. Using the pypi skeleton for `2.6.1` and this to build.sh.

```
#!/bin/bash

$PYTHON setup.py install

# Add more build steps here, if they are necessary.

if [[ `uname` == 'Darwin' ]]; then
     # change dynamic links to point to ssl.
     PG_LIB=$(pg_config --libdir)
     for LIBRARY in `find ${SP_DIR}/${PKG_NAME} -name "*.so"`;
     do
        install_name_tool -change libssl.1.0.0.dylib @rpath/libssl.1.0.0.dylib $LIBRARY
        install_name_tool -change libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib @rpath/libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib $LIBRARY
     done
fi
```

This really shouldn't happen - the chunking is done on document level (where we count the size of the metadata and the size of the document together). The main function responsible (`_chunk_actions`) returns a list of actions and only puts whole documents with their metadata into that list (see https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/helpers/__init__.py#L67-L69).

If you pass in an empty value for `id` argument the call will fail with `ValueError` due to the check in the method https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/client/__init__.py#L669-L671

Could you please verify that you have the latest version?

FYI, re: the `cryptography` dependency issue you referenced, I found a workaround by noticing that WebJobs will only accept wheels with an ABI tag of "`none`":
```
import pip
print(pip.pep425tags.get_supported())
[09/28/2016 17:08:30 > 775106: INFO] [('cp34', 'none', 'win32'), ('cp34', 'none', 'any'), ('cp3', 'none', 'any'), ('cp33', 'none', 'any'), ('cp32', 'none', 'any'), ('cp31', 'none', 'any'), ('cp30', 'none', 'any'), ('py34', 'none', 'any'), ('py3', 'none', 'any'), ('py33', 'none', 'any'), ('py32', 'none', 'any'), ('py31', 'none', 'any'), ('py30', 'none', 'any')]
```
Most modern wheels do not have that ABI tag, which is why `pip` will not accept the wheels for `cryptography==1.5.2`. Fortunately the wheel file can simply be renamed, e.g.
```
cryptography-1.5.2-cp34-cp34m-win32.whl =>cryptography-1.5.2-cp34-none-win32.whl
```
...and included along with a corresponding entry in the `requirements.txt` file. Will mention it on the other issue.

    def __repr__(self):
        return ''

display_handle.update(Invisible())
```

We could probably add a `DisplayHandle.clear()` method if that's useful.

Another way is to call `IPython.display.clear_output()`, but that removes all output from the current cell.

Finally, you could do it by processing the notebook file - e.g. it would be straightforward to load a notebook file, remove all png outputs, and save it again.

The code you have provided should work.  Just in case, I've provided an example below.  I suspect that the issue is your principal name.  Are you using this for HTTP SPENGO (i.e. [RFC 4559](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4559))? If so, notice that RFC 4559 specifies:

>  When the Kerberos Version 5 GSSAPI mechanism [RFC4121] is being used, the HTTP server will be using a principal name of the form of "HTTP/hostname".

So you should be using `HTTP/server.domain.com@DOMAIN.COM`, instead of `AUTH/server.domain.com@domain.com`.  Additionally, it's probably easier to use a keytab for your server instead of kinit-ing for it (extract the keytab to a secure place, and then use the  `KRB5_KTNAME` environment variable to make KRB5 use that keytab).

#### Example ####

Basic SPNEGO auth flow: https://gist.github.com/DirectXMan12/3f36666c26f0df4e024f

The code there is designed to be run under [gssapi-console](http://github.com/pythongssapi/gssapi-console), but that's only to properly set up the environment (i.e. the `REALM.xyz` parts at the beginning).  If you have an existing environment, you should be able to comment out the `REALM.xyz` lines and have that code work fine.

Just pushed a bunch more commits.  Again, these are best viewed commit-by-commit.  The ones this morning were intended to be obviously-correct, easy to review, and avoid making any serious design decisions.  In contrast, these meander and in some cases border on silly.

A few highlights:
- Implement `InvalidDatetimeError(ValueError)` and a couple others.
- Decrease the set of paths we can take through The Loop
    - Raise appropriate `ValueError`s instead of returning `(None, None)`
    - Remove `continue` statements and instead change a bunch of `if`s to `elif`s.  The cost of this is a few extra dictionary lookups.
    - Delay `i += 1` wherever possible.  Fewer state changes make reasoning easier (for me at least), and reduce the number of args/returns needed as pieces of the loop are refactored out into their own functions.
- Refactor out the `while True` loop into `_hms_block`.  In this case I still don't like the function it became (and don't like passing `res` or returning `i`), but separating it gives hope that it may be de-whiled.
- The silly one: override `l.__getitem__` to return an empty string for out-of-bounds access.  That way we can drop the `len_l` checks from a bunch of things like `if i+3 < len_l and info.month(l[i+3])`

The idea is that it would be really helpful if each step in the loop was just one function call, so we could step through it interactively.  Instead of doing that directly, this is just taking one step in that direction by separating out a big chunk of it.

We've got a few different threads going in which we're not 100% on the same page.  Let's put this on the backburner until we have some concensus-momentum going.

Before I get into a more detailed code review, I wanted to bring up a few concerns and propose some changes. `total_fraction_mapped_reads` maps to `% Reads Identified (PF)` in SAV. You can find it in the `index_lane_summary` here:
https://github.com/Illumina/interop/blob/51eb4ca8b2918b03e2c71d7c6d57a37914d9d0c3/interop/model/summary/index_lane_summary.h#L193


Basically, to get the information you require you need to populate either the `index_flowcell_summary`. You can do that in a manner very similar to the [summary example here](https://github.com/Illumina/interop/blob/master/docs/src/Tutorial_01_Intro.ipynb). Just replace run_summary with one of the aforementioned objects and call `summarize_index_metrics` instead of `summarize_run_metrics`.

Here is an example in C++ which is very similar
https://github.com/Illumina/interop/blob/master/src/apps/index_summary.cpp#L110

Also, I had forgotten that as of the latest release 1.0.25, we do support aggregating over all lanes in certain special cases.

The interface is a little different to do this (it is very new support).

If you pass in 0, instead of a lane number (>=1), then it aggregates over all lanes
summarize_index_metrics(run_metrics_object, 0, index_lane_summary_object);

You cannot get this information if you use the `index_flowcell_summary`.

I think it might just be because of using a sigmoid activation function with binary crossentropy. If the result is exactly 1 or exactly 0, the error function results in a NaN which ends up screwing the weights and future outputs.

Try using clipped results when calculating the error:

`loss = lasagne.objectives.binary_crossentropy(T.clip(predictions, 1e-7, 1-(1e-7)), target_var);`

If that doesn't work, it might be because the default activation function for dense layers is ReLU IIRC. The weights can get unrealistically high when the error is too big, which results in values too high causing NaN values.

To solve that, I would suggest scaling weights or weight updates to remain within a reasonable certain range.

You're missing the "channel" or "feature" dimension. Your input has a single channel (50 x **1** x 11025), so the 20 filters of your first convolutional layer have a single channel as well (20 x **1** x 400).
Because you have 20 filters, the input to the next convolutional layer has 20 features: l_conv_1.input_shape is (50, 20, something). So the 20 filters of your second convolutional layer process 20 channels each: (20 x **20** x 100).

It's the same for 2D convolution; the shape of `W` is always (num_filters, num_input_channels, all spatial filter dimensions).

@rhelmot I was writing a long and descriptive post to defend the proposed concept, but then I suddenly realized, that you're right! :D

It is not a "tool" concept that I was looking for. Actually, I was looking for a way to keep some intermediate data across different runs of the same analysis (of which I also thought as of a helper analysis). Because that data was completely opaque to the user and can only be understood by the analysis, I was trying to avoid an explicit passage of that data as an argument to the `__init__` proc as much as possible. So, I came up with a solution, which involves a new type of analysis, which was temporarily named as `IncrementalAnalysis`.

The main difference of the `IncrementalAnalysis` from the "classic" `Analysis` is that it is instantiated only once - upon its registration to the `AnalysisHub` - and is meant to be called using the python "call" syntax (i.e. via the `__call__` proc). A `IncrementalAnalysis` can store and access its internal state via the instance variables, as it is essentially a "singleton" and is created only once.

In fact, the only change that needs to be done is to adjust the `AnalysesHub._init_plugin()` method:

```python
# Add this.
class IncrementalAnalysis(Analysis):
    """
    Just a mixin.
    """

# Change base class to PluginHub.
class AnalysesHub(PluginHub):

    # Adjust this.
    def _init_plugin(self, plugin_cls):
        factory = AnalysisFactory(self.project, plugin_cls)
        return factory() if issubclass(plugin_cls, IncrementalAnalysis) else factory
```

A different naming convention could be used to distinguish the `IncrementalAnalysis` from the "classic" `Analysis`. For example, a `IncrementalAnalysis` could be named in lower-case, e.g. `project.analyses.my_helper_analysis`, while the "classic" `Analysis` should retain its current naming convention - the camel case naming - e.g. `project.analyses.MyComplexAnalysis`.

I'd like this shortcut functions because:
The use case to send a request and immediately wait for a response is really common (at least as soon as the lib is used to do something (trading, fetching information on demand, ...)). So having to write 3 lines to do this (without using exceptions) isn't really nice IMHO.
3 lines because wait_event could return None, so one **can't** simple do `foo, = wait_event()` because None is not iterable. So at least something like:
```
foo = wait_event(msg)
if not foo is None:
  foo = foo[0]
```
... would be necessary every time.

IMHO raising an exception for some cases when not receiving the answer is the correct thing to do, as for some requests a missing response IS an exceptional behavior. But i understand that it isn't required all the time, so i'd suggest adding an raise flag to the wait_for_message and wait_for_job functions so it behaves like `wait_event` but always return the message (or None) as a single response (not in a tuple).

I don't see any problem with the example you put. Based on the constructor, the user wants to train the effect of Li-Li and Li-O bonds on a certain output (e.g. band gap). The training data set is telling you that you have no pertinent data on how Li-Li and Li-O might affect the band gap. The warning in this case means that it cannot identify it to one of the scanner manufacturers for which we have specific code.
These are GE, Siemens and Philips and are actually only used for more compex MR sequence types like fMRI and DTI.

This warning just states that it is falling back to the "generic" conversion. This should be fine for just about all anatomical images (most CT and MR images). 

Possible reasons you get the warning:
- Manufacturer (0x0008, 0x0070) field is missing or empty in the dicom headers
- The manufacturer is not GE, Siemens or Philips

Unless you are encountering other issues this should not impact your conversion at all.

Agreed.  Looks like it's going to take nearly 30 GB to store the 1 million frames needed to reproduce the Nature paper on DQN (that's a rough figure).  It should only need 2^20 * 84^2 / 1024^3 = 6.89 GB when using PER.  I'm pretty sure it's because of the way `np.concatenate` is being used in `LazyFrames`  (https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/master/baselines/common/atari_wrappers.py#L197).  That's going to duplicate the frames over and over.  If instead `FrameStack` used a shape of `(4, 84, 84)`, then simply returning `[frame for frame in self.frames]`--and not using `LazeFrames`--would fix the memory problem. I'm not sure if keeping millisecond precision in all platforms and adding microsecond to selected platforms is the best solution. The MAC module should not set `otPlatformAlarm` directly because this peripheral is managed by the timer module. Microsecond API should be available in the timer mode as well. To achieve this architecture we would have to `#ifdef` a lot of code in the timer module (all microsecond related functions). That would be hard to read and to maintain.

I have one more idea that could help us reach a compromise between the platforms needs: 
`otPlatformAlarm` could implement platform specific timer frequency and it's API would support timer ticks unit instead of milliseconds or microseconds. The timer module would support milliseconds and microseconds in it's API and calculate number of timer ticks. With this approach Nordic timer could support microsecond timer precision while other platforms could use milliseconds. What do you think about this solution?

Hi Giedrius,

Yes, given OpenThread requires you to input shared network parameters to
start a network.  These pre-shared parameters minimally include the network
key and the xpanid.  I call this minimal set of parameters the Out-Of-Band
or OOB parameters.  Yes, it tends to be easier to setup a network when the
Mesh Local Prefix is derivable somehow, which has the added benefit of
keeping the minimum set of OOB parameters small. There are three ways to
remedy your problem:

1) include the Mesh Local Prefix in the OOB parameters used to setup nodes
A,B, and C.
2) derive the Mesh Local Prefix from some known parameter such as the
XPANID delivered in the Beacon.
3) get the Mesh Local Prefix during the attaching process.

Para futura referencia:
* `ErrorTree` tendrá como claves _únicamente_ aquellas claves del catálogo para las cuales hay errores, y sus padres. Por ejemplo, si el único error del catálogo está en catalog["publisher"]["name"],
  * error_tree["publisher"] y error_tree["publisher"]["name"] existirán, pero error_tree["title"] devolverá un error.

* Si el ErrorTree tiene un total de exactamente _cero_ errores, no importa qué clave se le pida, se resolverá correctamente (aunque no exista en la instacia validada) y devolverá un ErrorTree con total_errors == 0.

Actualmente, para saber la cantidad de errores totales a nivel "dataset", ejecutaba:
`dataset_total_errors = error_tree["dataset"].total_errors`

... que devuelve un error si no hay ningun error a ese nivel, pero sí en otras claves. La alternativa segura, consiste en lo siguiente:
```
if "dataset" not in error_tree:
    dataset_total_errors = 0
else:
    dataset_total_errors = error_tree["dataset"].total_errors
```

I don't see an easy fix to this (aside from changing all `object::borrowed` to `object::borrowed_t{}`): `borrowed`/`stolen` are constexpr declarations without definitions which can (typically when compiling without optimizations and/or in debug mode) trigger a linkage failure.  In C++17 we could add a `inline const object::borrowed_t object::borrowed;` definition, but in earlier C++ standards that's not allowed.  We could add a templated class (with a dummy template parameter) in between `object` and `handle` to allow an inline definition, but that's a bit messy.

The reason for the difference here is with ACMEv1, Certbot obtained each certificate in `fullchain.pem` separately and combined them together, but in ACMEv2, the ACME server provides `fullchain.pem` and Certbot splits it up to create `cert.pem` and `chain.pem`.

We definitely should remove the blank line from the top of `chain.pem`. We probably should also strip any blank lines found in `fullchain.pem` for consistency.

> Do you mean that the caller of settle() should provide all block headers since close() as arguments to the settle() function?

I don't think the idea is to provide _all_ block headers since close, but a sufficient number of headers where the sum of `available_gas` from these is enough for the other counter party to call unlock on all pending locks, proving that there was enough time to unlock any pending locks.

Ideally the gas slots proof would be in relation to the lock's expiration block instead of the channel's close block, since after the close block there could be lots of gas slots that couldn't be used with a given lock, because the secret is not revealed yet and it may be learned up to the lock's expiration. The meaning of the proof would be "for each of the pending locks, this is a proof of the first available gas slot since the lock expiration".

The above sort of implies that a "out of gas slot proof" should be added to the unlock function, the unlock transaction would contain a proof that it slipped after the expiration block because of the lack of gas slots, effectively extending the lock's expiration.

With that said, I think it's best to keep this complexity in the choice of the settlement period and lock's expiration instead of shifting it to the smart contract, the gas slot strategy basically allows for a smaller expiration values since the participants can provide a proof to extend the expiration period, instead of using a large expiration that accounts for the worst case (I'm assuming the out of gas slot thing), but I think the larger expiration periods is easier to implement and to reason about.

Lesson 1: automatic and transparent file transfer is very difficult.

There can be many cases:

1. Files reside on the server and there is no need to copy file over. They do not have to be `input` or `output` and might not have a corresponding valid path locally.

2. There can be a lot of results and `output` is just a representative of them. In this case the whole directory needs to be copied back.

3. Some variables need to be converted, and some do not. Automatic variable translation can be dangerous.

So in the end manual specification seems to be most readable and we only need to make the `specification` easier for users.

Yeah the problem with manually specifying is users don't actually understand what is happening.

This project was aimed to be a drop-in tool for developers that _don't_ understand packaging (tox is supposed to make that easy, right?).  Requiring users to understand how `setup.py` and `requirements.txt` and `-r`, what causes a rebuild, what doesn't, and etc. etc. interact isn't simple.  And at the end of the day, the knowledge for handling those is encoded in implementation details of `pip`!

Requiring a user to additionally duplicate all that information again in a configuration files not only tedious but incredibly error prone.

Also the overhead you're referring to here should be ~at most a second or two for an already created venv (comparable to the `pip freeze` call).  The real place where venv-update shines is when things change (which is pretty often for applications where bumping requirements is commonplace).  In this case venv-update knows how to uninstall and install packages to get to the proper desired state without throwing away the entire virtualenv.

import some_file

@PrinceKaro wrote: 

> I do not have widows update on currently

Of course, **you** are the ruler of your machine, but running an unpatched OS 
and connecting to the internet with it leaves you vulnerable to attacks made possible 
because loopholes at the OS level have been made public when Microsoft posted their 
updates to patch said loopholes... 
This threat is still real even if all apps you are using have been updated to their latest 
versions and you are running a fully up-to-date Security suite on the machine...

> partially due to frustration on how long it takes to update on my computer

I am the maintainer of my sister's Win7SP1 x64 laptop (she knows nada beyond 
facebook and twitter), indeed what you said had been plaguing the Win7 OS for 
many months, but they have fixed it now by distributing un updated "Update Agent" 
for Win7; visit Dalai's latest [page](http://wu.krelay.de/en/2016-11.htm) and download manually the updates 
on the **Win7 x86** column (that is if you are indeed on 32bit); 
make sure that wuauserv isn't running (Task Manager -> Services Tab) 
and install those downloaded .msu updates manually 
(double-clicking and waiting), one by one, followed by a reboot between them; 
if you are met with trouble, read troubleshooting instructions inside 
Great White North's post in this [link](https://answers.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/forum/windows_vista-update/updates-not-working-it-has-been-searching-for/92cd6922-17f6-4730-b46b-91a480b95dd3) - it's for Vista initially, but same principle apply. 

After you have successfully installed the updates on Dalai's page, 
re-enable Windows Update, reboot, then launch **Check for Updates**;
it shouldn't take more than two hours to find all Important 
and Recommended. BTW, Microsoft have also changed the format of updates 
on Win7+ and instead of many standalone ones they are now 
issuing cumulative ones (Rollups); so it isn't that bad now... 

> due to worry that microsoft would try and foist 
> windows 10 upon my computer without permission

Not applicable now; the free Win7 => Win10 update offer 
expired at the end of last July (29th to be exact), so no more 
GWX stuff is pushed through Windows Update currently. 

> due to the ungodly amount of time it would take for my computer to catch up

Read above...

> (running the standalone update doesn't work)

How so? if it seems to have stuck forever at the "Searching..." stage, 
then, again, read above; plus, KB2999226 has SP1 as a prerequisite...

> but when it should cease to function 
> I now know what I likely need to do 
> to get streamlink running.

Make yourself and everyone else a favour 
and fully update your OS soon, rather than later; 
don't let it become yet another bot!

> thank you all for the time you took out of your days to help

You are welcome; this is an Open Source project, 
all work involved is purely on a voluntary basis...

Sorry to comment on a closed issue, but I've been struggling for hours to make the textarea auto expand to the height of its parent container.  Buttons do it, bees do it - but not the text area.

```
from ipywidgets import Layout, Button, Box
outer = Layout(display='flex', height='100px', border='solid')
inner = Layout(flex='1', height='auto')
items = [widgets.Textarea(layout=items_layout, rows=None) for i in range(2)]  # rows=1 fails too
box = Box(children=items, layout=outer)
items[0].value = ''.join('{:.>15}'.format(n) for n in range(5, 1450, 5))
box
```

`items = [widgets.Button(layout=items_layout) for i in range(2)]`
they **do** resize vertically nicely.

And in pure HTML/CSS outside of Jupyter, the textarea does grow to the enclosing height see my fiddle https://jsfiddle.net/tcab/syv2spq2/, so its possible, but something is blocking this behaviour in Jupyter?  Or am I missing something?

> Is that everything you want to do with widget states? Is restarting the kernel necessary? Is clearing cell outputs before clearing widget state necessary?

I don't think restarting a kernel closes all of the active widgets - I think they stick around in the frontend (sort of orphaned, but still providing models and views to the outputs).

Perhaps any kernel restart should do a 'close all widgets' on the frontend, which will automatically clear them from the outputs.

And..... crud. You're right. I totally forgot that `@fixture` pretends to be like `@contextmanager` in most ways, *except* that it doesn't give access to exceptions.

So... ugh. I know why pytest did it this way: their conceptual model is that when they setup/teardown fixtures, that can raise an exception and they can handle it and attribute it to that fixture, and when they run a test, that can raise an exception and they can handle it and attribute it to that test. But that model breaks down in the face of concurrency: if a fixture is running code in the background, then it can fail at any moment, including while a test is running, and in that case the thing to do is to unwind out of that test -- marking it as incomplete or errored or something -- and give up on any other tests that use that fixture, and then report the error in the fixture. That's just the facts of life. The way Trio encodes those facts into Python code is that if a fixture's background task fails while a test is running, it injects a `Cancelled` exception into the test, which is supposed to unwind all the back out through pytest's machinery until it reaches the fixture. And if pytest were built to support async/await and Trio natively, that's how it would work, and it would make perfect sense for fixtures to act like context managers – you'd end up with an effective call stack like: `trio.run` → pytest fixture management code → fixture → pytest test management code → test, and exceptions would bubble out until they reached the point that could handle them: most would be caught by the test management code, but `Cancelled` would keep going.

That's not how pytest works though.

It could be how *our* fixtures work, I guess – we could make `@trio_fixture` work like a context manager, but only allow it to see `Cancelled` exceptions. (Or `MultiError`s containing nothing but `Cancelled` exceptions.) I'm worried though that that would be dangerously confusing, since people are used to fixtures like

```python
@fixture
def tempfile():
    name = create_tempfile()
    yield name
    os.unlink(name)
```

and that'd be subtly broken if you forgot and used the same pattern for a `@trio_fixture`, because you'd have to instead write:

```python
@trio_fixture
def tempfile():
    name = create_tempfile()
    try:
        yield name
    finally:
        os.unlink(name)
```

In a perfect world with infinite resources I guess we could reimplement pytest to work the way we want, but in this world I think maybe we better stick to the pytest way of doing things, even if it's kinda broken... the alternative just seems too error prone.

So if we can't do the "obvious" thing, then I guess this suggests that we should do two things:

* Forbid fixtures from yielding inside cancel scopes (which includes nurseries). There's some discussion of this in https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/264, but basically the tl;dr is that we probably can't solve this in general without first adding features to the interpreter, so this would need some pytest-trio specific hack to catch. (I think implementing this will need a bit of help from trio proper -- in particular some way to ask trio whether a new cancel scope has been opened. Maybe a `trio.hazmat.cancel_scope_depth` function that we could call before and after initializing the fixture to check if it changed?)

* Provide a special magical `fixture_nursery` fixture which fixtures can use to spawn background tasks, and which automatically gets cancelled at some point – after fixture cleanup, I guess.

Does... that make sense? I feel like I've been completely wrong about this stuff several times now so I'm wondering what I missed this time :-)

IDDatatype:
    t=ID
;
```

When I try to recognize: `model 45 { kind test_kind }`, PyEcore throw me a `BadValueError: Expected type INT(int), but got type str with value test_kind instead`. I'm not sure, but I think it should make the difference between the `IDDatatype` from `a` and the one from `c`. In the context of `b`

```python
mclass = metamodel[node.rule_name]
